coredata.dc:description: In excess, cobalt and lead ions adversely affect both the environment and ecosystems, having negative impact on agricultural plants and feed cultures as well as fish, animal, and human health. In this study, a three-component sorbent based on carbon dots (CDs), hydroxypropyl methyl cellulose (HPMC), and silica (SiO2) was developed for the removal of cobalt(II) and lead(II) ions from aqueous media. CDs synthesized directly on the HPMC surface were condensed with SiO2 via the hydrolysis of tetraethyl siloxane. As a result, a recyclable porous CD/HPMC/SiO2 nanosorbent with high sorption capacity was synthesized. The CD/HPMC/SiO2 nanosorbent has an adsorption capacity of 258 ± 4 mg/g and 168 ± 6 mg/g for lead(II) and cobalt(II), respectively. The material also displayed adequate cyclability as Co2+ and Pb2+ ions were easily removed from the sorbent surface.

SEM images of the (a) SiO2 reference sample and (b) CD/HPMC/SiO2 sorbent; (c) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm according to relative vapor pressure; (d) pore size distribution; (e) FTIR spectra of SiO2 reference (red), HPMC (green), CD/HPMC (blue), and CD/HPMC/SiO2 (black). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Emission spectrum of sorbent dispersed in pure water and stock solutions (excitation wavelength 350 nm); (b) FTIR spectra of CD/HPMC/SiO2 after batch adsorption experiments (solid lines) and regeneration (dotted lines) compared to the original sample.
(a) EDX mapping of CD/HPMC/SiO2 + Co2+; (b) mapping of sample CD/HPMC/SiO2 + Pb2+;

(a) Maximum adsorption capacity in water enriched with different metal ions CD/HPMC/SiO2 before and after batch adsorption with Co2+, Ni2+, Cr3+ (experiments performed for mono-component solutions); (b) sorbents before and after its exposure in metal ions solutions under natural light and under UV-exposure (batch adsorption capacity experiments were replicated triple).

Comparative analysis of conditions that influence adsorption capacity: (a) temperature, (b) contact time, (c) pH, and (d) concentration of CD/HPMC in reaction mixture, (e) removal efficiency for Co2+ after sorption – regeneration cycles, (f) removal efficiency for Pb2+ after sorption – regeneration cycles. Data presented in (a)-(d) was obtained as a result of experiments replicated triple.

head.ce:title: Silica/carbon dot nanosorbent for the detection and removal of Pb(II) and Co(II) ions from wastewater

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Energy Lab, ITMO University, 9 Lomonosova Street, St. Petersburg 191002, Russian Federation
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Energy Lab, ITMO University, 9 Lomonosova Street, St. Petersburg 191002, Russian Federation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In excess, cobalt and lead ions adversely affect both the environment and ecosystems, having negative impact on agricultural plants and feed cultures as well as fish, animal, and human health. In this study, a three-component sorbent based on carbon dots (CDs), hydroxypropyl methyl cellulose (HPMC), and silica (SiO2) was developed for the removal of cobalt(II) and lead(II) ions from aqueous media. CDs synthesized directly on the HPMC surface were condensed with SiO2 via the hydrolysis of tetraethyl siloxane. As a result, a recyclable porous CD/HPMC/SiO2 nanosorbent with high sorption capacity was synthesized. The CD/HPMC/SiO2 nanosorbent has an adsorption capacity of 258 ± 4 mg/g and 168 ± 6 mg/g for lead(II) and cobalt(II), respectively. The material also displayed adequate cyclability as Co2+ and Pb2+ ions were easily removed from the sorbent surface.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Current challenges to sustainable environmental development include reducing the amount of heavy metal ions found in aquatic environments. Heavy metals adversely affect the health of living organisms, including humans, leading to chronic poisoning and organ failure as well as cancer and other genetic diseases [1–4]; thus, their removal is vital. Due to their widespread use in the manufacture of many common materials, such as several alloys, glasses, paints, car batteries, electronics, and perovskites, lead and cobalt are two of the most common pollutants discharged in industrial wastewater, oftentimes contaminating various water sources, including rivers, ponds, lakes, and groundwater [5,6].

Because water sources must be kept clean, water purification, including heavy metal ion removal, remains a significant area of applied research. Heavy metal ions can be removed by a wide number of methods. Membrane-based methods (e.g., filtration, osmosis, and electrodialysis) use size discrimination to separate heavy metal ions from water, preventing large particles from passing through the membrane [7–9]. Although cost-inefficient, membrane separation remains one of the most popular approaches to heavy metal ion removal [10,11]. Chemical precipitation, another conventional wastewater treatment method, precipitates metal ions via chemical bonding to remove them from aqueous environments [11–16]. Despite its high efficiency, the chemical agents used in this process may result in secondary pollution [10,13,15]. Photocatalysis is another frequently used approach for the removal of heavy metal ions and is based on redox chain reactions proceeding on the photocatalytic carrier. While photocatalysis can be used to treat industrial-grade systems, it is pH-dependent and is inefficient with mixed metal ions solutions [17–23].

Studies have shown that adsorption is a reliable approach to reducing the amount of heavy metal ions in aqueous environments [24–26]; as such, various sorbents with excellent ion selectivity, high performance, and high recovery rates have been developed [27–29]. However, conventional sorbents are expensive and require environmentally unfriendly synthesis, limiting their application [25,30–33]. Recently, sorbents made from ecologically friendly materials have attracted considerable interest [26,34,35]. Such sorbents have performances, recovery rates, and selectivities that rival more traditional sorbents like ion-exchange resins, activated carbons, and synthetic polymers. Moreover, their simple, ecological, and cost-effective synthetic methods make them suitable next-generation sorbents. Among all sorbent materials, silica- and carbon-based composites have been particularly useful in purification [25,29,35–37].

As well known for their sorption of organic as for inorganic pollutants, silica nanoparticles have been developed for the purification of aqueous media or for the selective capture of organic dyes, proteins, drugs, radionuclides, and some heavy metal ions [27,38,39]. Pure silica nanoparticles intrinsically have relatively low sorption properties; however, these properties are usually improved through the addition of surface functional groups, which utilizes organic syntheses to obtain silica-organic compounds and increases the cost of the resulting sorbent [40,41]. Carbon dots (CDs) are a large class of quasi-spherical nanoparticles with a carbon core and various functional groups on its shell [42–44]. Depending on synthesis conditions, different functional groups can be incorporated into the shell, including carboxyl, hydroxyl, amine, amide, and nitro groups [45–47]. While their ability to form complexes and their ease of production enable a variety of applications in wastewater treatment, including sorption [42,48,49], CDs are difficult to separate from water – which may result in secondary pollution by nanoparticles [49,50]. It is hypothesized that functional group-rich CDs could be bonded with silica nanoparticles to create a sorbent with high sorption capacity. The functional groups present on the CD surface can be used to cost-effectively improve the sorption capacity of silica, which would, in turn, act as a support material for the CDs, allowing them to be easily removed from aqueous environments [51–53]. Another advantage of CDs is their excellent optical properties, which has been used across several industries for various applications, including wastewater management, where it has been used as a detector. CDs are naturally fluorescent materials that convert ultraviolet (UV) light to visible light upon irradiation. Studies have shown, that CDs can be used to confirm the presence of heavy metals, which effectively quench CD fluorescence by forming complexes [54,55].

In the present work, we present an easy-to-prepare, cost-effective, novel CD/silica nanoparticle sorbent for the detection and sorption of cobalt and lead ions in wastewater. The proposed sorbent shows high capacity for both heavy metal ions and can be easily regenerated, being reusable for a minimum of three cycles.

Branched polyethyleneimine (PEI, Mw ∼ 800 by LS, average Mn ∼ 600 by GPC) and hydroxypropyl methylcellulose (HPMC, viscosity 2600 – 5600 cP in 2 % H2O) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36.5 – 38 %), ammonia water solution (23–25 %), iron(III) nitrate (Fe(NO3)3), 99 %), and copper(II) nitrate (Cu(NO3)2, 97 %) were purchased from LenReaktiv. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥99 %), nitric acid (HNO3, 1 M), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, anhydrous, ≥99 %), sodium chloride (NaCl, anhydrous, ≥99 %), cobalt(II) nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O, ≥99 %), nickel(II) nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, ≥98 %), zinc(II) nitrate (Zn(NO3)2, 98 %), chromium(III) nitrate nonahydrate (Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, 99 %), and lead(II) nitrate (Pb(NO3)2), 98 %) were obtained from LenReaktiv. L-tyrosine (C9H11NO3, 99 %) was supplied by Acros Organics. Mercuric nitrate (HgNO3, 98 %) was obtained from Chemmed. Milli-Q-grade deionized water (DI, 18.2 M Ω cm resistivity) was used in all experiments.

CD/HPMC was synthesized under reflux. Briefly, 0.6 g HPMC and 0.2 g PEI were dissolved in 10 mL Milli-Q grade water. After complete homogenization of the solution, the mixture was transferred to a 50 mL two-neck round-bottom flask. Before heating, the reaction medium was purged with Ar for 30 min. Then, the solution was heated at a constant temperature of 120 °C for 4 h under vigorous stirring. Thus, a suspension containing carbon dots on the surface of HPMC was obtained. Dialysis was used to remove organic residues and low-molecular carbon structures that did not bind to the carbon dot surface during synthesis. Dialysis was conducted for 24 h with the use of deionized water and a 3.5 kDa membrane with the water being changed every 2 h. The cleaned product was then freeze dried. As a result, water-soluble foam-like material was obtained and prepared for further analysis and sorbent preparation.

CD/HPMC/SiO2 sorbents were synthesized using a modified Stober process [56]. Briefly, 20 mL of EtOH was added to 3 mL of TEOS in a glass vial (Solution A) under vigorous stirring. In another vial (Solution B), dried CD/HPMC powder was dispersed in 2 mL EtOH at different concentrations (from 2.5 % to 50 % wt; the weight ratio of CD/HPMC:SiO2 was 1:2.8, 1:3.5, 1:4.7, 1:7, 1:14, 1:28, 1:56) to determine the CD/HPMC:silica ratio at which adsorption capacity is maximum. Afterwards, enough concentrated HCl to lower the pH of the solution to 3 was added to the vial with CD/HPMC under stirring. According to the determination of optimal CD/HPMC concentration mentioned in the part devoted to sorption experiments, all experiments had a 1:7 ratio to Solution B (20 wt%). Then, after 15 min of stirring, Solution B was added dropwise to Solution A. Hydrolysis and co-polycondensation of silanols and CD molecules were performed at room temperature under continuous stirring (450 rpm) over 4 h. Finally, 92 μL of ammonia water solution (23 wt%) was added to the mixture, and a gel-like product was obtained after 5 min. The gel was aged for 6 h before being purified via dialysis in deionized water for 24 h using a 3.5 kDa membrane with the water being changed every 2 h. After dialysis, the suspension was lyophilized for further characterization and sorption capacity analysis. For the SiO2 control, the same procedure was used but instead of adding CD, an equivalent volume of water was added.

The hydrodynamic diameter of CD was determined via dynamic light scattering (DLS). The zeta (ζ) potential was measured with the use of laser Doppler electrophoresis (LDE). Both measurements were performed on a Compact particle size analyzer (Photocor, Russian Federation) at 25 °C in a disposable capillary cell. The morphology of the sorbent was investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) using a VEGA3 scanning electron microscope (Tescan, Czech Republic) with an X-Act energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) detector attachment. Lyophilized samples mixed with KBr crystalline (1:5 w/w ratio) were pressed into pellets for Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy analysis, which was conducted on a Nicolet iS5 FTIR spectrometer (ThermoFisher Scientific, United States) between 4000 – 500 cm−1 at 4 cm−1 increments. UV–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy for CD suspension analysis and calibration curve evaluation for metal solutions were performed on a Cary 60 UV–Vis spectrometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). Photoluminescence (PL) was analyzed on a Cary Eclipse spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). The photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) was determined by comparing measurements of CD suspensions with an L-tyrosine standard. Nitrogen adsorption–desorption, which was performed at 77 K on a NOVA 1200e gas sorption analyzer (Quantachrome Instruments, USA), was used to study the surface area and pore size as well as to determine the adsorption isotherms. The surface areas of the samples were determined using Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) theory. The FTIR spectra of CD/HPMC, CD/HPMC/SiO2, CD/HPMC/SiO2 with adsorbed cobalt and lead, and CD/HPMC/SiO2 after desorption were collected using a Nicolet iS 5 FTIR spectrometer (ThermoFisher, USA).

To determine the effect of heavy metal ions on CD photoluminescent properties, stock solutions of metal ions with a concentration of 1000 ppm (mg/L) were prepared. An aliquot from the stock solutions was mixed with aqueous sorbent suspension to achieve a 10-fold excess of metal ion solution in volume. To avoid potential effects caused by changes in pH, the pHs of all samples was set to 5. To find the (I0-I)/I0 ratio, the values of the integral intensity under 360 nm excitation were compared.

To determine the photostability of CDs and sorbent suspensions, the samples were continuously illuminated under different conditions: 350 nm using a 25 W xenon lamp in the Cary Eclipse spectrophotometer, UV lamp (360 nm, 120 W), domestic light (28 W), and under direct natural sunlight (at least 8 h a day) for 14 days, with further comparison of their spectra.
To determine the thermal fluorescence stability of CDs and adsorbent, both suspensions were heated at 80 °C for 24 h and every hour an aliquot was taken to analyze the normalized integral intensity.

Metal ion adsorption from solutions was performed under static conditions (batch adsorption) in order to investigate the adsorption capacity of the sorbent. For the series of experiments, 0.01 g CD/HPMC/SiO2 was mixed in a flask with 10 mL of Co(II), Pb(II), and Ni(II) solutions (1000 mg/L) with the use of a water bath shaker. The resulting solution was then centrifuged to remove sorbent scattering particles, and the residual concentration of ions in the filtrates was afterwards measured via UV–Vis spectroscopy and their pH was measured by pH-meter after adsorption.

To determine the effect of temperature on adsorption capacity, samples were incubated at different temperatures. Contact time tests were performed in order to define the moment at which equilibrium between solid and liquid phase occurs. To investigate the effect of pH on adsorption capacity and sorption mechanism, the sample was shaken with metal ion solutions between pH 1 and 7, which were obtained by adding NaOH or HCl into media. The adsorption capacity of the sorbent was also measured with varying CD concentrations in the sorbent at the moment of CD/HPMC/SiO2 preparation.

In all experimental runs, the adsorption capacity (qe, mg/g) was calculated using Eq. (1):
(1)
q e
=
C 0
-
C e
×
V M
where
C 0
and
C e
are the initial and final concentration of ions in solution (mg/L), respectively;
V
is volume (L); and
M
is the mass of CD/HPMC/SiO2 used. The batch adsorption experiments were conducted in triplicate.
The influence of salinity on adsorption was analyzed by preparing 1000 ppm Co(II) and Pb(II) solutions in NaCl-enriched water (from 0.5 g/L to 30 g/L) and then taking batch adsorption measurements.

To prove the adsorption selectivity, a sample with the same (1000 ppm) concentration of two interfering ions was prepared. We selected different ion pairs to determine in which case the priority of adsorption can be observed. The pairs we chose are Co(II)/Ni(II), Co(II)/Cu(II), Cu(II)/Pb(II), Ni(II)/Pb(II), and Co(II)/Pb(II). For Co(II)/Ni(II) and Co(II)/Cu(II), we compared their absorbance at 510, 655, and 350 nm for Co(II), Ni(II) and Cu(II), respectively (pH = 5 for all experimental runs). For Cu(II)/Pb(II), Ni(II)/Pb(II), Co(II)/Pb(II), it was difficult to provide such an experiment due to limitations caused by impossibility of masking cobalt without further masking of lead. After the batch adsorption experiment, the sorbent was removed from solution by centrifugation. To mask and determine the lead quantity adsorbed, an equivalent amount of 1000 ppm SO4 2- ions were added to the solution (from a stock SO4 2- standard). Then, the sample was centrifuged and the precipitate was collected, filtered, and then weighed in order to determine the quantity of lead ions. With all proper recalculations for weight and volume of solution, cobalt (II) concentration were determined via conventional UV–Vis methodology. The experiments for the metal ion pairs were conducted in triplicate.

For the regeneration study, desorption batch experiments were performed. The used sorbent was separated from the salt solution used in the batch adsorption experiments and then added to 10 mL of deionized water, NaOH solution (0.1 M), and HNO3 (0.1 M). Subsequently, after 2 h of contact between the regenerative media and the used sorbent, the concentration of metal in the obtained solution was measured. In order to examine the sorbent’s reusability, the regenerated sorbent was redispersed in metal ion solution (1000 mg/L), and the operation repeated.

Dynamic light scattering was used to determine the hydrodynamic diameter of the obtained materials. The size of the synthesized CD/HPMC (pH = 7.86, C = 0.01 g/mL) was determined to be 206 ± 12 nm while that of the sorbent (CD/HPMC/SiO2) was 420 ± 24 nm with narrow size distribution (Fig. S1). Differences in the ζ potentials of CD/HPMC and the sorbent were measured at different pHs. The relationship between ζ potential and pH is presented in Fig. S2. For CD/HPMC, ζ potential drastically decreases as pH increases from 1 to 7 from −13.33 ± 1.26 to –32.26 ± 2.02 mV. As for the sorbent, only a slight decrease in ζ potential was observed with increasing pH (from + 6.12 ± 0.34 mV to + 5.14 ± 0.26 mV). The values for the SiO2 reference are a + 11.57 ± 3.35 mV and + 13.21 ± 0.88 mV for pH = 7 and pH = 1, respectively. These changes in ζ potential indicate that chemically active groups are present on the sorbent surface.

The observed changes in ζ potential with changing pH may be attributed to the relative proportions of carboxylic and amine groups in the compounds. The negative ζ potential of CD/HPMC could be mainly attributed to deprotonated carboxyl groups on the surface, and condensation with silica resulted in a decrease in negativity. As a result of the condensation between the carboxyl groups on the CD/HPMC surface and the hydroxyl groups of the silanols, the relative contribution of the protonated amino groups to the ζ-potential increased [28,57].

To confirm the formation of CDs on the HPMC surface, the sample was analyzed by EDX mapping and compared with HPMC before the synthesis (Fig. S3). The formation of nitrogen-doped sites distributed throughout the HPMC surface indicates the formation of CDs on the polymer surface.

The light absorption (Fig. 1
a) and PL spectra (Fig. 1 b,c) of CD/HPMC were measured. CD/HPMC synthesized in dilute aqueous solution had absorbance peaks at 270 and 350 nm (Fig. 1 a), which corresponded to π-π* and n-π* transitions of the carbonyl groups of CD/HPMC, respectively. Photoluminescence excitation and emission showed strong luminescence in the blue-green band (400 – 520 nm) after having been excited at 320 – 380 nm. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of CD/HPMC were 350 nm and 470 nm, respectively. Under UV light, the CD/HPMC water solution fluoresced greenish-blue.

To determine the photostability of CD/HPMC, the sample was irradiated under a 350 nm xenon lamp for 2 h. The results showed that CD/HPMC retained 95.5 % of its original integral fluorescence intensity with shift of peak less than 0.1 nm, indicating high resistance to photobleaching. In addition, the emission spectra of freshly-prepared CDs were compared to samples exposed for 14 days under domestic light, UV lamp, and natural sunlight; the samples retained 97.2 %, 94.2 % and 94.3 % of its original integral fluorescence intensity, respectively, without significant shift of peak (0.3–0.5 nm). The thermal stability of the CD sample also shows negligible changes in integral fluorescence emission under 350 nm excitation and in peak shift (0.25 nm). Summarizing, comparison between all samples in different conditions are shown in Fig. S4 .

SEM analysis indicated that CD/HPMC/SiO2 (Fig. 2
b) had a complex porous foam-like microstructure with different pore sizes and shapes, which also occurred in the SiO2 reference (Fig. 2 a).

The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm and pore size distribution of the CD/HPMC/SiO2 sample are presented in Fig. 2 c. The isotherm corresponds to type IV of the IUPAC classification, which is associated with mesoporous materials. The hysteresis can be explained by adsorption metastability (delayed condensation due to the metastability of the adsorbed multilayer) or by pore network effects affecting desorption [58]. Nitrogen adsorption showed a sharp increase at a low relative pressure of approximately 0.45p/p0. The surface area was calculated to be 420 m2/g. The total pore volume, measured at a relative pressure (p/p0) of 0.99, was determined to be 0.107 cm3/g. Pore diameter distribution results showed a propagating peak at 3 – 4 nm, indicating that mesopores with diameters of 3 – 4 nm are most common (Fig. 2 d).

The FTIR spectra of SiO2, HPMC, CD/HPMC, and CD/HPMC/SiO2 (Fig. 2 e) were measured. The spectrum of the pure silica sample had a peak at 1083 cm−1, which could be attributed to the stretching vibration of Si–O–Si. The same peak appeared in the CD/HPMC/SiO2 composite with a shift, which indicates changes in the strength of molecular interactions – indicating structural and compositional changes. The CD/HPMC sample displayed several peaks that were observable in the sorbent sample and were responsible for the sorption process: the peaks at 1638 cm−1 and 1056 cm−1 could be assigned to C = N and C–N bonds, respectively; the peak at 2840 cm−1 corresponded to O–H bonding; and the broad band at 3460 cm−1 could be attributed to O–H and N–H bonding. Moreover, the peaks at 3460, 2840, and 1467 cm−1 in CD/HPMC and 3348 and 1412 cm−1 in CD/HPMC/SiO2 may be attributed to the presence of carboxylic groups. Significant differences between the HPMC and CD/HPMC FTIR spectra are due to a large set of complex transformations during carbon dots formation; however, the most evident difference is the appearance of a peak at 1046 cm−1, which may be attributed to C–O group stretching caused by breaking of the HPMC polymer chain and possible reaction between PEI and HPMC at hydroxyl groups in the glucopyranose unit. The FTIR spectrum of the CD/HPMC/SiO2 composite sample had several peaks that indicated the presence of chemical heterolytical Si–O–C bonding. The bands at 1273 cm−1 and 971 cm−1 corresponded to Si–O–Ar bonding. The Si–O–C bond consisted of C–O and Si–O bonds and appeared between 950 and 1320 cm−1.

The number of amine and carboxylic groups on the surface of CD/HPMC and, therefore, the CD/HPMC/SiO2 sorbent, directly depend on CD synthesis conditions. Thus, differences in the number of functional groups quantity should resulted in a peak attributable to sorption due to electrostatic interactions between metal ions and the chemical nature of sorbent surface.

As the degree of –NH2 protonation decreased with pH, CD/HPMC/SiO2 ion affinity also increased significantly before pH = 5. Conversely, an increase in pH led to the conversion of free R–COOH groups into carboxylates, which also leads to adsorption. As such, sorbent capacity relies on a balance between –NH2 protonation and –COOH conversion. Batch adsorption experiments showed that the point of balance between these processes was pH = 5 for Co(II) and pH = 6 for Pb(II), at which the highest adsorption rates were achieved. The difference between this point for two metals is explainable due to differences in their complex formation constants [59–61].

The sorption of lead and cobalt was also confirmed by IR spectra of samples after batch sorption tests (Fig. 3
b); mostly, these metals tend to form amino and hydroxyl complexes, which is indicated by a decrease in peak intensity (3348, 2975, 2894, 1644, 1412 cm−1). Insignificant decreases in the intensity of the 950 and 820 cm−1 peaks also indicated adsorption onto Si-OH groups, but according to the integral peak intensity changes, the adsorption on this active site is negligible. The shift peaks (3348 to 3346 cm−1, 1644 to 1640 cm−1) with intensity change after sorption might indicate a mixed sorption, where the process of sorption via hydroxyl and amine groups and the entrapment of metal ions in porous structure of SiO2 both contribute [62,63].

After six sorption-regeneration cycles, we observed insignificant decrease in peak intensity responsible for amino groups (0.2–1 % from its original integral peak intensity), which indicates that the slow washing of functional carbon nanostructure leads to a further reduction in adsorption capacity. The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms were measured for the CD/HPMC/SiO2 regenerated samples (Fig. S5). The isotherms were similar, with a sharp increase at the same relative pressure, and the surface areas for the regenerated samples were calculated to be 401 m2/g for the cobalt(II) regenerated sorbent and 387 m2/g for the lead(II) regenerated sorbent. The total pore volume, measured at a relative pressure (p/p0) of 0.99, were determined to be 0.093 cm3/g and 0.089 cm3/g for cobalt(II) and lead(II) regenerated sorbents, respectively, with significant shift in mesopore size. The changes observed in the summary may reflect the changes in the adsorbent after the regeneration processes, which are mainly caused by diffusion resistance processes in the pores and less likely by chemical degradation of the surface [64,65].
For PL determination of CD/HPMC/SiO2, the emission spectra under 350 nm extinction wavelength were measured in the absence and presence of heavy metal ions (Fig. 3 a).

Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis was used to determine the distribution of metals on the sorbent surface after exposure in aqueous stock solutions (Fig. 4
c,d). EDX mapping showed that the surface was enriched with metal ions.

The stability of the fluorescence under continuous exposure to light is of further important practical significance, as it offers the possibility of monitoring and quantifying the intensity on measuring devices and analytical laboratory equipment. As with the carbon dots, experiments were carried out on the stability of the sorbent ( Fig. S6 ). The sorbent retained 98.1 % of its original integral fluorescence intensity. The sorbent sample showed much greater resistance to fading than carbon dots due to the shell provided by silica, which is consistent with studies on the stability of fluorophores in composites with SiO2 [17,67]. Comparison of the integral intensity of the fresh sample and the sample exposed to household light, UV lamp and natural sunlight for 14 days: the samples retained 99.2 %, 98.1 %, and 97.1 %, respectively, with no significant shift in the peak (0.3–0.5 nm).

The thermal stability of the sorbent sample was analyzed in the same way as CD/HPMC. Changes in integral fluorescence emission were much less than in case of CDs (less than 0.2 % of quenching), which emphasizes the influence of silica-based shells on stability of optical properties (Fig. S6). A peak shift was not observed or located under the threshold of equipment deviation (less than 0.05 nm). In summary, the optical activity of the sorbent is stable and resistant to photobleaching from excess optical emission, including monochromatic light, and resistant to temperature changes with continuous treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heavy metal ions detection and influence of adsorption on photoluminescence properties
ce:section.ce:para:
After adsorption, the quenching of fluorescence for sorbent material occurs (Fig. 5
a,b). Due to the presence of a large number of hydroxo-, carboxyl- and amine- functional groups on the surface of the final composite, the sorption capacity increases significantly. However, only CDs are responsible for photoluminescence in the composite sorbent [66]; as such, the authors believe it is correct to consider the interaction between CDs and target-ions. The mechanisms of quenching of CDs are extremely complex, there are few main mechanisms of photoluminescence quenching: static, dynamic, photoinduced electron transfer (PET), inner filter effect (IFE), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), Dexter energy transfer (DET), and surface energy transfer (SET) [67].

According to modern concepts, the interaction of lead (II) with carbon dots obtained from different sources leads to the formation of a complex in which CDs are the donor and Pb2+ ions are the electron acceptor, i.e., the mechanism of Photo-induced electron transfer (PET) is realized [67,68].

Considering the sorption mechanism as the formation of a complex between metal ion and sorbent as a ligand, the occurrence of static quenching occuring may also be explained by formation constants. For static quenching, chelation effects provided by CD are described by the Benesi-Hildebrand equation and double logarithmic chelation equation [69]. However, according to the mathematical model, most of the metal should be adsorbed before cobalt. The inversely proportional tendency in quenching/sorption ratio shows that, in the case of iron, zinc, nickel, and copper, dynamic quenching prevails. The obtained results of fluorescence measurements implied that quenching caused by the interaction between CD and metal ions do not correspond with adsorption capacity values. For example, despite the comparatively notable quenching ratio (more than 0.8) for nickel (II), zinc (II) and copper (II) ions (Fig. 5 a), the highest adsorption capacity observed was 12 mg/g, 23 mg/g, and 33 mg/g, respectively, indicating a preferential dynamic quenching mechanism without complex formation as can be seen from example of the sorbent after exposure to the nickel (II) ions stock solution (Fig. 5 b). Interactions between cobalt (II) and lead (II) and the sorbent resulted in a comparatively high quenching rate; however, the sorbent had an adsorption capacity of 161 mg/g and 255 mg/g for cobalt (II) and lead (II), respectively.

According to modern concepts, the interaction of Pb2+ with CDs obtained from different sources leads to the formation of a complex in which CDs are the donor and Pb ions are the electron acceptor, i.e., the mechanism of photo-induced electron transfer (PET) is realized. In earlier research, it was shown that CDs are recovered by pyrophosphate ion after quenching as a result of lead sorption in view of the higher affinity of lead to pyrophosphate ions and, as a consequence, the formation of a new complex with it [68,70].

The mechanism of interaction between cobalt and CD is most likely of mixed nature. The absorption spectra of cobalt ions and CD emission coincide, which causes an internal filter effect. But it is also possible that excited electrons are transferred to cobalt, filling its 3d-orbital [71,72].

The PLQY was measured for CDs and the sorbent and reached 24 % and 21 %, respectively, when compared to L-tyrosine as a standard, which means fluorescence could be easily observed. The qualification analysis of the presence of heavy metal and the quantification of the adsorption capacity of the derived sorbent can be done by analyzing samples on measuring devices. The practical significance of PLQY lies in the possibility of analyzing it with an unequipped eye, using only UV light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different parameters on adsorption
ce:section.ce:para: It is widely known that under different conditions the surface properties and, consequently, the adsorption capacity of a sorbent may change drastically [39,55,73].

The adsorption capacity of CD/HPMC/SiO2 seemed to gradually decrease with increasing temperature, as shown in Fig. 6
a, which was due to the fact that adsorption is an exothermic process. The most favorable temperature at which sorption was optimal was determined to be 15–20 °C. The sorption capacity of CD/HPMC/SiO2 was found to increase with shaking time, reaching equilibrium after 5 min. Furthermore, short contact time would be one of the most essential concerns for economical applications in wastewater treatment. From the results obtained, an optimum agitation period of 60 min was selected for Co(II) and Pb(II) removal for subsequent batch adsorption tests to ensure that equilibrium was achieved. The curve shown in Fig. 6 b indicates that two steps were involved in the adsorption process: a slower step followed a rapid initial one. The adsorption capacity of CD/HPMC/SiO2 for Co(II) was determined to be 110 ± 6 mg/g within 100 s, after which it slowly plateaued, and the maximum value was established as 161 ± 7 mg/g. For Pb(II), equilibrium was achieved within 186 s, at which the adsorption capacity reached 255 ± 9 mg/g.

The pH of the solution influenced the adsorption capacity of CD/HPMC/SiO2 due to its effect on the surface characteristics of the adsorbent and dissociation or ionization of the adsorbate. Adsorption onto CD/HPMC/SiO2 was investigated between pH 1 and 7, as illustrated in Fig. 6 c. The removal of cobalt and lead increased as the pH increased to 5.

The amount of CD/HPMC dispersed in ethanol during the adsorbent synthesis also played an important role in the adsorption capacity of CD/HPMC/SiO2. As Fig. 6 d indicates, the 20 wt% CD/HPMC was the most suitable sorbent for both Co(II) and Pb(II) ions; while the 30 wt% CD/HPMC sorbent had a slightly higher Co(II) capacity, it adsorbed less Pb(II) ions. Sorption capacity decreased with increasing CD/HPMC concentration after 30 wt%, confirming that 20 wt% CD/HPMC concentration in ethanol dispersion during synthesis is optimal. The decrease in CD/HPMC/SiO2 adsorption capacity could be explained by the nature of the interaction between silica and CD/HPMC. Essentially, the sorbent acquired more active centers responsible for the adsorption of metal ions. However, given the polymeric structure of HPMC and the fact that the CD/HPMC surface consisted of a wide variety of functional groups, the active sites of the sorbent may be blocked due to chemical interaction between the functional groups [74].

The effect of salinity on Co(II) and Pb(II) adsorption were analyzed in NaCl enriched water (Fig. S7). The limit of efficiency for the sorbent in high-saline media is 25 g/L NaCl (15 mg/g for Co(II) and 5 mg/g for Pb(II)), which corresponds to the salinity of sea water [75].

The maximum adsorption capacity for Co(II) (162 ± 6 mg/g) was achieved at 20 °C, pH = 5, t = 300 s, and 20 wt% CD/HPMC concentration in reaction volume during the synthesis of CD/HPMC/SiO2. The maximum adsorption capacity for Pb(II) (258 ± 4 mg/g) was achieved at 15 °C, pH = 6, t = 190 s and 20 % CD/HPMC concentration in reaction volume during synthesis of CD/HPMC/SiO2.

Additional tests for unfavorable environmental conditions were performed. The conditions for sorption were 60 °C and pH = 1 and the batch experiment lasted for 1 h. The performance of CD/HPMC/SiO2 dropped to 112 ± 11 mg/g for Co(II) and 94 ± 9 mg/g for Pb(II). Compared with optimal results, the general decrease in performance was by approximately a half.

The sorbent regeneration was found in acidic media. The removal efficiency for Co(II) ions remained more than 90 % for at least four cycles (Fig. 6 e). For Pb(II) ions, the same removal efficiency was observed after three cycles (Fig. 6 f).

The adsorption selectivity studies in the presence of two interfering cations for pairs Co(II)/Ni(II), Co(II)/Cu(II) and Co(II)/Pb(II) showed excellent selectivity to cobalt ions. For Co(II)/Ni(II) and Co(II)/Cu(II), Co(II) adsorption capacity was 136 ± 2 and 158 ± 5 mg/g, respectively. At the same time, nickel and copper ion adsorption capacity in these conditions were 8 ± 3 mg/g and 5 ± 1 mg/g, respectively. For Pb-related pairs, Ni(II)/Pb(II), Cu(II)/Pb(II), the same relatively low results for nickel (12 ± 2 mg/g) and cooper (10 ± 1 mg/g) were observed. Pb(II) adsorption capacity in the presence of interfering Ni(II) and Cu(II) cations reached 230 ± 1 mg/g and 212 ± 5 mg/g, respectively. The sorbent performance for Co(II)/Pb(II) showed selectivity to cobalt (132 ± 7 mg/g and 11 ± 1 mg/g for Co(II) and Pb(II), respectively). The obtained results show the priority of sorption for cobalt and lead in the presence of different heavy metal ions.

Due to its influence on adsorption, the influence of interfering anions on adsorption was studied. Taking into account the precipitation of heavy metals in the presence of anions and in accordance with the effect of salinity on adsorption capacity, the influence of NaCl content in water on adsorption should be analyzed [57–59]. The limit of efficiency for the sorbent in high-saline media comes up to 25 g/L NaCl dissolved (15 mg/g for Co(II) and 5 mg/g for Pb(II)) which corresponds with salinity of seawater [60].

After sorption, the pH of cobalt and lead nitrates solutions were additionally measured. The change in pH from 5.03 to 5.56 for cobalt and from 4.13 to 4.93 for lead in solutions without any ions provided by pH adjustment is related to a decrease in heavy metal concentration due to the formation of chelate-like complexes. In neutral media, the pH changed from 7 to 7.15 for cobalt ions and to 7.22 for lead ions. No precipitation of heavy metal salts or complex compounds was observed.

Additionally, to add details to the sorption mechanism, pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order models were fitted (Figs. S8, S9). According to the calculations of correlation coefficients, the most fitting model for cobalt (R = 0.968) and lead (R = 0.997) is pseudo-first order. The pseudo-second order model also shows significant correlation coefficients (for cobalt R = 0.957, for lead R = 0.972). Such correspondence may indicate a mixed adsorption mechanism, provided by functional groups on the CD surface and by porosity and surface area of SiO2, which also includes diffusion in sorbent grains [76–81].

The adsorption isotherms of Co(II) and Pb(II) were investigated to get a more comprehensive insight into the mechanism of adsorption and maximum adsorption capacity of heavy metal ions onto CD/HPMC/SiO2. From the adsorption isotherm, the data acquired was fitted to the linear form of Langmuir equation:

The data obtained was also fitted to the linear form of the Freundlich isotherm and presented in the logarithmic form in correspondence with the following equation:

The adsorption data can be additionally described by the Dubinin–Radushkevich isotherm to differentiate between physical and chemical adsorption. It does not assume a homogenous surface or a constant adsorption potential; therefore, it is more general than the Langmuir isotherm. According to the equations used to describe the Dubinin-Radushkevich isotherm [82], the value of the adsorption free energy, which determines whether adsorption is chemical (
E
= 8 – 16 kJ/mol), or physical (
E
< 8 kJ/mol) [82,83], was far less than 8 kJ/mol: 1.01 and 0.89 kJ/mol for Co(II) and Pb(II), indicating that the adsorption of Co(II) and Pb(II) ions onto CD/HPMC/SiO2 was physical. However, the results of analysis by FTIR does not corresponds with such determination. Based on this fact and the mathematical analysis of equation at ‘solid-liquid’ interfaces, other methods were used to confirm the isotherm data [84,85].

The results of isothermal fitting and corresponding constants are presented in Table 1 . The R2 was highest in the Langmuir model, indicating that the Langmuir hypothesis of monolayer coverage of the surface is the most fitting in the case of the CD/HPMC/SiO2 sorbent. Adsorption isotherms are presented in Fig. S10
and
Fig. S11 .
The comparison of the adsorption capacity values towards Co2+ and Pb2+ of CD/HPMC/SiO2 and other reported sorbents is displayed in Table 2 .

The industrial use of sorbents is usually associated with dynamic flow-through adsorption, where the water stream flows through the sorbent load. A desorption process should also be realized in which a 0.1 M HNO3 solution (regenerant) flows through the load after it has reached the limit of adsorption capacity to recover a concentrated metal solution. Different schemes could be realized here: batch process and countercurrent process [98–100].

The batch process is a common approach for utilizing adsorption phenomena in various industrial processes. In this method, the feed stream is passed through a fixed bed containing the adsorbent until it is saturated with the most adsorbed compound. Once saturation is reached, the adsorbed compound can be effectively removed by using temperature, pressure or inert displacement methods [99,100].

To increase the efficiency of the system and minimize the time loss associated with the cycle change, a continuous countercurrent process can be used. In this approach, the adsorbent is continuously circulated between two beds, allowing simultaneous adsorption and desorption steps [98,99].

According to the schemes, the concept can be proposed for CD/HPMC/SiO2 on an industrial scale. First, in dynamic adsorption experiments where the water stream flows through the sorbent loading, a desorption process should be carried out where a 0.1 M HNO3 solution (regenerant) flows through the loading after it reaches the limit of adsorption capacity to recover a concentrated metal solution. In order to achieve a continuous process for the recovery of metal ion brine, a parallel scheme of desorption should be realized: the water stream splits to two sorbent loadings, and when one of them reaches the limit of adsorption capacity (determinable with the increase of concentration in the outlet by a meter), the inlet stream is overridden, and then the regenerant stream is turned on. At the same time, the inlet flow to the second sorbent load is switched on and this inlet change is continued before the entire load should be replaced.

The system can also be combined with domestic quality water treatment systems, but pilot studies should be carried out for this case as well as for the previously described ones in order to determine the optimal parameters of the water flow for an effective adsorption process.

Highly effective sorbents for selective lead and cobalt sorption have been synthesized in this work. The maximum adsorption for lead(II) and cobalt(II) is 258 ± 4 mg/g and 168 ± 6 mg/g, respectively. Under unfavorable conditions, working performance is halved, which is a fine competitive result. We have created a reusable, inexpensive, effective CD/HPMC/SiO2 hybrid sorbent for the removal of heavy metal ions from aqueous environments even under unfavorable conditions. Adsorbed metals can be easily removed from the surface of CD/HPMC/SiO2 using acidic media for regeneration. In consideration of the high recyclability of 3 cycles, the obtained sorbent is an effective tool for selective purification from Co(II) and Pb(II).
